content:
It Starts Small

3 min read
Just now

--

You open a blank document.
You don’t write the first sentence.
You wait.
For a suggestion.
For a prompt.
For a “better way” to say it.
And slowly, without noticing,
you stop starting thoughts.
You start selecting them.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image is Generated by AIThe Shift Nobody Announced
We like to think AI is just a tool.
Like a calculator.
Like search.
But something deeper is happening.
We’re not just using AI.
We’re beginning to think through it.
Researchers are already pointing to this shift as “cognitive offloading”, where mental effort is transferred to external systems instead of being done internally.
At first, it feels like relief.
Less effort.
Less friction.
Faster output.
But thinking was never supposed to feel effortless.
From Thinking… to Choosing
There was a time when thinking looked like this:

• Struggling to phrase a sentence

• Sitting in confusion

• Rewriting ideas multiple times
Now it looks like:

• picking from suggestions

• slightly editing outputs

• approving what “sounds right”
That subtle shift matters.
Because:
Thinking creates ideas.
Selecting filters them.
And we’re doing more filtering than creating.
The Illusion of Intelligence
Here’s the dangerous part:
You still feel smart.
You produce:

• better emails

• cleaner documents

• faster results
But research shows something uncomfortable:
Frequent reliance on AI tools is linked with reduced critical thinking effort, especially when people accept outputs without questioning them.
Even more concerning:
People often believe they’re improving
while their independent reasoning quietly weakens.
So the output improves.
But the internal process?
It slowly disappears.
The Suggestion Loop
AI doesn’t just answer.
It suggests:

• How to write

• How to think

• How to structure ideas
And over time, those suggestions become defaults.
This creates what researchers describe as AI dependency, where thinking shifts from internal reasoning to AI-assisted patterns.
You don’t notice it immediately.
But eventually:

• Your ideas start sounding similar

• Your structure becomes predictable

• Your thinking follows familiar patterns
Not because you lack creativity
but because:
You’re operating inside someone else’s suggestions.
The Loss We Can’t Measure Easily
This isn’t about becoming “less intelligent.”
It’s about losing something harder to track:

• original thought

• mental struggle

• creative friction
Studies suggest AI use can reduce deep cognitive engagement and encourage superficial learning instead of effortful thinking.
And that friction?
That uncomfortable process of figuring things out?
That’s where real thinking lives.
The Workplace Version of This
You see it everywhere now:

• presentations that sound perfect… but feel empty

• strategies that look smart… but lack depth

• people who execute fast… but struggle to explain why
Experts are calling this a growing “cognitive divide”,
where most people rely on AI to think for them, while a small group uses it to think with them.
The difference is subtle.
But massive.
Why This Feels So Good (And That’s the Problem)
AI removes:

• uncertainty

• delay

• cognitive effort
It gives you clarity instantly.
And your brain loves that.
Because thinking is expensive.
It requires:

• energy

• time

• discomfort
So naturally
You outsource it.
But Here’s the Trade-Off
When you stop generating thoughts:

• Your curiosity weakens

• Your memory becomes external

• Your confidence becomes dependent
Not because AI is harmful.
But because:
Your brain adapts to what it doesn’t need to do anymore.
The Real Question Isn’t About AI
It’s about you.
Are you:

• Using AI to extend your thinking?

• Or replacing your thinking with it?
Because both are happening.
At the same time.
Final Thought
You’re not becoming less intelligent.
That’s not the problem.
The real shift is quieter:
You’re no longer starting thoughts from scratch.
You’re starting from suggestions.
And over time
that changes not just what you think.
But how do you think?
